start.scenario: It's 45°F, light rain, no shelter, no fire-starting gear. You have 90 minutes of daylight. What do you do first?

options.text: Build a debris hut immediately
Search for natural shelter (cave, rock overhang)
Gather dry tinder and look for ways to make fire

debris_hut.scenario: Good. You start building. You find enough dry leaves and branches. But the ground is wet. Do you:

options.text: Build a raised debris platform to sleep on
Sleep directly on the leaf pile

natural_search.scenario: You spend 30 minutes searching and find a shallow rock overhang. Not deep enough to fully block wind. Do you:

options.text: Use it as a windbreak and pile debris around yourself
Keep searching for something better

fire_first.scenario: Without fire-starting gear this is risky. 45 minutes pass with no fire. Daylight is almost gone. You now have no shelter and no fire. This was the wrong priority.
fire_first.explanation: In wet, cold conditions without fire gear, shelter is always the first priority. Hypothermia kills faster than hunger or thirst. Fire is secondary when you have no reliable way to start one.

good_outcome.explanation: Correct. Ground insulation is critical — cold ground pulls heat from your body faster than cold air. A raised platform or thick debris layer between you and the ground is essential. You survive the night.

bad_outcome_cold.explanation: Ground conduction would pull heat from your body all night. Even with debris above you, sleeping directly on wet ground leads to hypothermia. Always insulate from below first.

bad_outcome_dark.explanation: The overhang was adequate. Continuing to search costs time and energy — you run out of daylight with no shelter at all. The first viable option is almost always the right option.

scenario: You're building a debris hut for a 40°F night. Which single layer of your debris hut is most critical for preventing hypothermia?

options.text: A thick roof layer to block rain (minimum 2 feet of debris)
The debris packed on the floor beneath you — minimum 6 inches of dry material
A leaf-stuffed door plug to block wind at the entrance
Leaves piled on the outside walls to a thickness of 18 inches

explanation: Ground conduction is the #1 heat thief in shelter scenarios — the earth will pull body heat away 25x faster than cold air of the same temperature. Six inches minimum of dry debris BELOW you is non-negotiable. Roof thickness matters for insulation and rain but is secondary to floor insulation. The door plug prevents wind intrusion but convective loss is less severe than conductive. A good rule: build your floor first, thick enough that you cannot feel the ground through it, then build the roof. If you can only do one, do the floor.

scenario: You need to select a campsite for the night. Rank these site selection criteria from HIGHEST to LOWEST priority.

items.text: No widow-makers (dead branches or standing dead trees) overhead within falling range
A natural windbreak on the prevailing wind side (rock outcrop, dense conifers)
Flat ground for sleeping — sloped sites cause you to slide and disrupt sleep
Water source within 100 meters for morning needs
Natural concealment from sightlines (depression, dense brush)

explanation: A dead branch overhead can kill you in your sleep — this is always #1. No other criterion matters if a widow-maker falls on you. Windbreak is #2 because convective heat loss during sleep is a serious hypothermia risk, and wind-driven rain can destroy any shelter. Flat ground is a comfort and sleep quality concern — not immediately life-threatening but severely impacts performance. Water proximity is a morning logistics concern — you can haul water once. Concealment is tactical and relevant in grid-down scenarios but not a primary survival priority vs physical threats.

scenario: You've built a quinzhee (mounded snow shelter) in winter emergency conditions and have been inside for 3 hours with 2 other people. What is the single greatest risk that could kill you overnight?

options.text: Frostbite from contact with the snow walls as body heat melts the surface
The roof collapsing from snow weight accumulation above
Ventilation hole becoming blocked — CO2 buildup leading to suffocation
Moisture from breath condensing and re-freezing, dropping interior temperature

explanation: CO2 (not CO — no combustion source) buildup from breathing is the primary killer in snow shelters. Three people in a sealed quinzhee can reach dangerous CO2 levels (above 5%) in under 2 hours if the vent hole is blocked. The vent hole must be maintained — poke it clear from inside every time you check it. Mark the vent location outside with a ski pole or stick so rescuers can find it. Frostbite from walls is minor — the interior surface warms above freezing from body heat. Collapse is rare with a properly hardened quinzhee (let it sinter 90 minutes before hollowing). Moisture is an insulation/comfort issue, not a life threat.

start.scenario: 3 days after a full grid-down event. Mid-January, outdoor temps at night: 18°F. You are in a 6-story apartment building. Power, heat, and water are out. You have: 2 sleeping bags (rated to 20°F), a propane camp stove with 1 canister, a hand-crank radio, and food for 5 days. Where do you shelter?

options.text: Stay in your 4th-floor apartment and work to retain heat
Move to a ground-floor unit (empty, can break in) for easier exit/entry
Move to the building's stairwell — it's interior and protected

stay_4th_floor.scenario: Smart. Upper floors retain heat longer than ground-floor units due to reduced floor conduction and wind exposure. Which heat retention action do you take first?

options.text: Seal the apartment door and windows with duct tape and plastic sheeting to reduce air infiltration
Move all sleeping gear into one interior bathroom and create a 'room within a room'

seal_room.explanation: Sealing reduces infiltration significantly. However, the most effective urban cold-weather technique is the 'room within a room' — concentrate body heat in the smallest interior space possible. The large exterior window is a major thermal bridge. That night, your 580 sq ft apartment drops to 38°F — survivable in sleeping bags, but cold. Better execution: move to interior room + use sleeping bags together.

interior_room.explanation: Excellent. The 'room within a room' principle is the most effective passive heat retention in grid-down urban scenarios. Interior rooms with no exterior walls (typically bathrooms and closets) lose heat only through interior walls, which are insulated against other units. Body heat in ~40 cubic feet combined with sleeping bags rated to 20°F creates a survivable microclimate even in severe cold. The bathroom stays 20°F warmer than the exterior apartment.

go_ground.explanation: Ground-floor units have floor conduction from the cold earth, greater wind infiltration at door seals, and are the first-floor breach point in insecure conditions. The slab foundation conducts cold directly — ground temperatures in January may be 32-36°F, turning the floor into a massive heat sink. Upper floors with interior rooms are dramatically warmer. Stay above the second floor whenever possible.

stairwell.explanation: Stairwells are the worst possible shelter in a cold apartment building. The chimney effect — warm air rising and escaping through roof access, drawing cold air in at the base — creates a continuous cold draft. Without walls on all sides, no heat can accumulate. The stairwell may be useful for brief movement but never for sleeping in cold conditions. Stay in enclosed units with closable doors.
